---
title: "LLM-assisted projects: practical tips"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
---

A short set of working principles I've converged on after a stretch of building R packages, dashboards, and analyses with Claude Code as a daily collaborator. None of these are novel — they're cribbed and consolidated from people much further along — but they're the ones that actually changed how I work.

## The shape of the day

**Plan more than you execute. 60% planning, 40% execution.**

Counter-intuitive at first because LLMs are so fast you assume more execution = more output. In practice the opposite holds: an LLM can implement a clear spec in minutes but struggles for hours against a fuzzy one. Time spent sharpening the spec pays back several times over, because the fast part stays fast and the slow part (rework, blind alleys, conflicting context) shrinks.

The pattern that works for me:

1. **Sketch what you want.** WHAT, not HOW. Success criteria, not step-by-step instructions.
2. **Hand the sketch to a fresh LLM and ask it to tear the spec apart.** Identify points that are underspecified, inconsistent, or weird. Iterate until the remaining questions are genuinely niche.
3. **Hand the sharpened plan to a new LLM with a simple prompt.** "Implement this." If you've done step 2 well, the simple prompt is enough.

The rationale for separating step 2 from step 3 is that the LLM doing the critique should not be the one doing the build — different framings, different blind spots.

## Declarative beats imperative

Tell the LLM what success looks like, not which buttons to press.

- **Imperative:** "Read file X, parse the JSON, filter to dates after Y, write to Z."
- **Declarative:** "Produce a CSV at Z containing only rows from X where date > Y. Tests should cover empty input, malformed JSON, and the date boundary."

Declarative prompts let the LLM bring its training data to bear. Imperative prompts pin it to your particular sequence and waste that.

This is also why **writing tests first** is so productive in this workflow: tests are declarative by nature. They state success, not procedure. If you can write the test, the LLM can write the implementation.

## Parallel execution

Multiple models, multiple worktrees, in parallel:

- A cheap fast model (Haiku) handles mechanical edits — typos, renames, version bumps.
- A mid-tier model (Sonnet) handles per-file work — debugging, single-feature implementation.
- A larger model (Opus) handles synthesis, architecture, and conversation with you.

Use `git worktree add` so two sessions can work on independent branches without stepping on each other. The orchestrator session decides what to delegate; the worker sessions just do the work.

The cost gradient matters more than people expect. Doing all your work at the top tier when most of it is mechanical is the single biggest budget leak.

## What you lose

Worth being honest about:

- **You lose the ability to write code from a cold start.** The muscle atrophies if you don't exercise it. I now find myself reaching for the LLM for things I could have typed in 30 seconds, and the cumulative effect on craft is real.
- **You ship code you have not read.** Even with diff review, the volume eventually exceeds what you can scan attentively. The remedy is mechanical: tests, types, code review, and a willingness to revert. The remedy is not "read every diff"; that's a stage you grow out of.

Neither is a reason not to do this work. They're costs to acknowledge so you don't pretend they don't exist.

## What's covered elsewhere

Reproducibility (Nix, rix, locked dependencies) is mandatory regardless of LLM use. The rest of this site, and the [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/JohnGavin/llm/blob/main/AGENTS.md) file at the repo root, document those practices in detail.

```{r build-info}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
sha <- tryCatch(substr(system("git rev-parse HEAD", intern = TRUE), 1, 7),
                error = function(e) "N/A")
cat(sprintf(
  "---\n\nllm | [Git %s](https://github.com/JohnGavin/llm/commit/%s) | R %s | Built %s\n",
  sha, sha, paste(R.version$major, R.version$minor, sep = "."),
  format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
))
```
